Summary Add a progress percentage and module count display to the installer output so users can see how far along the installation is. ## Implementation Details ### Add progress tracking globals In install.sh or a dedicated lib: \`\`\`bash # Progress tracking declare -g TOTAL_MODULES=0 declare -g CURRENT_MODULE=0 declare -g INSTALL_START_TIME=0 # Initialize at start of installation init_progress() { INSTALL_START_TIME=\$(date +%s) # Count modules from manifest or hardcode TOTAL_MODULES=13 # shell, lang, tools, agents, etc. CURRENT_MODULE=0 } \`\`\` ### Create progress display function \`\`\`bash show_progress() { local module_name="\$1" CURRENT_MODULE=\$((CURRENT_MODULE + 1)) local percent=\$((CURRENT_MODULE * 100 / TOTAL_MODULES)) local elapsed=\$(($(date +%s) - INSTALL_START_TIME)) local elapsed_min=\$((elapsed / 60)) local elapsed_sec=\$((elapsed % 60)) # Calculate progress bar local filled=\$((percent / 5)) # 20 chars total local empty=\$((20 - filled)) local bar="" for ((i=0; i44px), Glossary clear button (32px->40px). All other components pass mobile UX standards."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4jr","title":"Implement handle_checksum_mismatch() with skip/abort/proceed options","description":"# Task: Implement handle_checksum_mismatch() with batched prompts ## Purpose When an upstream installer checksum does not match, provide user options while maintaining security. ## Critical UX Improvement: Batch All Mismatches **Problem with per-tool prompts:** If 5 tools have changed, user gets 5 separate prompts. Annoying and breaks flow. **Solution:** Collect all mismatches first, then present ONE decision prompt. ```bash declare -a CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES=() # During verification phase (collect, dont prompt) check_installer_checksum() { local tool="$1" url="$2" expected="$3" local actual=$(fetch_checksum "$url") if [[ "$actual" \!= "$expected" ]]; then CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES+=("$tool|$url|$expected|$actual") return 1 # Mark as mismatch but continue fi return 0 } # After all tools checked, present batched decision handle_all_checksum_mismatches() { if [[ ${#CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES[@]} -eq 0 ]]; then return 0 # All good fi echo "" log_warn "Checksum mismatches detected for ${#CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES[@]} installers:" echo "" for entry in "${CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES[@]}"; do IFS="|" read -r tool url expected actual <<< "$entry" echo " $tool:" echo " Expected: ${expected:0:16}..." echo " Actual: ${actual:0:16}..." done echo "" echo "This usually means upstream scripts were updated." echo "" echo "Options:" echo " [P] Proceed with new versions (update checksums.yaml later)" echo " [S] Skip these tools, install everything else" echo " [A] Abort installation" echo "" read -r -p "Choice [P/s/a]: " choice case "${choice,,}" in s) for entry in "${CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES[@]}"; do SKIPPED_TOOLS+=("${entry%%|*}") done return 0 ;; a) exit 1 ;; *) return 0 ;; # Proceed (default) esac } ``` ## Non-Interactive Mode In non-interactive (agent/CI) mode, behavior depends on tool classification: - CRITICAL tool mismatch → abort (cant proceed safely) - RECOMMENDED tool mismatch → auto-skip with warning ## Security Considerations - Always log the actual vs expected hash - Encourage users to verify upstream changes before proceeding - Document how to update checksums.yaml ## Dependencies - Depends on: Tool classification (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-v8a) - Used by: verify_and_run() flow (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-anq) ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] All mismatches collected before any prompt - [ ] Single batched prompt for all mismatches - [ ] Clear display of which tools affected - [ ] P/S/A options with sensible defaults - [ ] Non-interactive mode follows CRITICAL/RECOMMENDED rules","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:44:37.23646-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:46:15.314163-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:46:15.314163-05:00","close_reason":"Added checksum mismatch batching to security.sh: CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES array, record/clear/count/has functions, handle_all_checksum_mismatches() with P/S/A prompt, check_installer_checksum(), and _handle_mismatches_noninteractive() for CI/agent mode. {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9ntj","title":"TASK: Create /wizard/verify-key-connection page","description":"## Parent Feature agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-t66c (Wizard Password-First Flow Updates) ## Summary Create a new wizard page that guides users to verify their SSH key-based connection works after the installer has set it up. This closes the loop on the password-first flow and proves the key installation succeeded. ## Rationale After the installer prompts for and installs the SSH key, users need to: 1. Disconnect from the password-based session 2. Reconnect using their SSH key 3. Verify it works without password This step is crucial because: - Confirms key was installed correctly - Teaches user the "correct" connection method - Provides troubleshooting if key doesn't work ## Page Content ### Header "Verify Key-Based Connection" ~1 min ### Main Content \`\`\`tsx // Step 1: Disconnect

Step 1: Disconnect

Type 'exit' and press Enter to close your current session:

Your terminal should return to your local computer prompt.

// Step 2: Reconnect with key

Step 2: Reconnect with your SSH key

Now connect using your SSH key instead of password:

// Success indicator
  • No password prompt appeared
  • You see: ubuntu@vps:~$
If you had to enter a password, something went wrong - see troubleshooting below. \`\`\` ### Troubleshooting Section Common issues and fixes: 1. **Still asks for password** - Key may not have been pasted correctly during installer - Re-run installer: it will prompt for key again 2. **Permission denied (publickey)** - Key file permissions might be wrong - Run: \`chmod 600 ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519\` 3. **Connection refused** - VPS may have rebooted - Wait 1-2 minutes and try again ### SimplerGuide Content Explain: - Why we verify this step - What "key-based authentication" means - Why no password is good (more secure, more convenient) ## Technical Details ### File Location \`apps/web/app/wizard/verify-key-connection/page.tsx\` ### Step Number This becomes step 8 in the new flow (after run-installer). ### URL \`/wizard/verify-key-connection\` ### Navigation - Previous: run-installer (or status-check) - Next: accounts ### State Requirements - Needs vpsIP from localStorage (same as ssh-connect page) ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] New page exists at /wizard/verify-key-connection - [ ] Shows exit command first - [ ] Shows key-based SSH command - [ ] Success criteria clearly stated - [ ] Troubleshooting for common key issues - [ ] SimplerGuide explains the "why" - [ ] Proper navigation (prev/next) - [ ] Step number updated in wizardSteps.ts ## Dependencies - TASK: Update /wizard/ssh-connect (page flow order) - TASK: Integrate SSH prompt into installer (key must be installed first) ## Files Modified - Fix to use double-escaped backslashes so the copied command is valid on Windows.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"FuchsiaCreek","created_at":"2025-12-20T14:03:29.105181-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T14:04:03.163713-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T14:04:03.163713-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed Windows command strings in generate-ssh-key step to properly escape backslashes (so copied commands use /Users/jemanuel\.ssh\... instead of dropping slashes)."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9xt","title":"[ubu.1] Create ubuntu_upgrade.sh library","description":"# Create ubuntu_upgrade.sh Library ## Purpose Core library implementing Ubuntu upgrade detection and execution logic. ## File Location `scripts/lib/ubuntu_upgrade.sh` ## Functions to Implement ### Version Detection ```bash # Get current Ubuntu version as comparable number (e.g., 2404 for 24.04) ubuntu_get_version_number() # Get current Ubuntu version string (e.g., "24.04") ubuntu_get_version_string() # Compare two version numbers (returns 0 if $1 >= $2) ubuntu_version_gte() { local v1="$1" v2="$2"; } ``` ### Upgrade Path Calculation ```bash # Get next available upgrade version (queries do-release-upgrade -c) ubuntu_get_next_upgrade() # Calculate full upgrade path from current to target # Returns newline-separated list of versions ubuntu_calculate_upgrade_path() { local target="$1"; } # Get the latest available Ubuntu version ubuntu_get_latest_available() ``` ### Pre-upgrade Checks ```bash # Run all pre-upgrade validations # Returns 0 if all checks pass, 1 with error details if not ubuntu_preflight_checks() # Individual checks (called by ubuntu_preflight_checks) ubuntu_check_disk_space() # Need ~5GB free ubuntu_check_network() # Test connectivity ubuntu_check_apt_state() # No broken packages ubuntu_check_not_docker() # Upgrades don't work in containers ``` ### Upgrade Execution ```bash # Perform single-version upgrade # Returns 0 on success, 1 on failure ubuntu_do_upgrade() # Prepare system before upgrade (apt update, dist-upgrade) ubuntu_prepare_upgrade() # Setup resume mechanism for after reboot ubuntu_setup_resume() # Cleanup resume mechanism after completion ubuntu_cleanup_resume() ``` ## Dependencies - Sources: logging.sh, os_detect.sh - External: do-release-upgrade, apt, lsb_release ## Design Decisions ### Why query do-release-upgrade instead of hardcoding? Hardcoding version numbers (like "25.10") becomes stale. By querying `do-release-upgrade -c` (check mode), we always know the actual next available upgrade. This future-proofs the code. ### Why check disk space? do-release-upgrade downloads hundreds of MB of packages and needs space for temporary files. Running out of disk mid-upgrade is catastrophic. We require 5GB free (conservative estimate). ### Why not use unattended-upgrade? unattended-upgrade is for security patches within a version, not for full distribution upgrades. do-release-upgrade is the official tool for version upgrades. ## Testing Notes - Test on 22.04, 24.04, 24.10 base images - Verify upgrade path calculation is correct - Ensure preflight checks catch common issues","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:19:52.130051-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:32:12.108731-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:32:12.108731-05:00","close_reason":"Created ubuntu_upgrade.sh with all core functions: version detection, upgrade path calculation, preflight checks, and upgrade execution. The bead was created 1 minute after the fix was committed."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dmff","title":"Add headless browser auth flow explanation","description":"# Task: Add Headless Browser Auth Flow Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Authentication Flow for Headless VPS (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3edj) ## Description When users run `claude` or `gh auth login` on the VPS, these tools try to open a browser. But there's no browser on the VPS! The tool prints a URL that must be opened on the user's laptop. This "device flow" is completely unexplained. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/status-check/page.tsx Add explanation before the auth commands: \`\`\`tsx

Your VPS doesn't have a web browser, so authentication works a bit differently:

  1. Run the login command (like claude)
  2. The terminal will display a URL (or code)
  3. Copy that URL and paste it in your laptop's browser
  4. Complete the login in your browser

**Immediate retry (0s):** Many transient errors clear instantly (TCP reset, brief DNS hiccup) - **5 second wait:** Enough for most CDN/routing issues to resolve - **15 second wait:** Handles rate limiting, brief outages - **Total wait:** 20 seconds max - fast enough to not frustrate users ```bash RETRY_DELAYS=(0 5 15) # Immediate, then 5s, then 15s retry_with_backoff() { local cmd="$1" local description="$2" local max_attempts=${#RETRY_DELAYS[@]} for ((attempt=0; attempt 0)); then log_info "Retry $attempt/$((max_attempts-1)) for $description (waiting ${delay}s)..." sleep "$delay" fi if eval "$cmd" 2>/dev/null; then ((attempt > 0)) && log_info "Succeeded on retry $attempt" return 0 fi done return 1 } ``` ### Retryable Errors Only retry for transient network issues: ```bash is_retryable_error() { local exit_code="$1" local stderr="$2" # curl exit codes for network issues case "$exit_code" in 6) return 0 ;; # Could not resolve host 7) return 0 ;; # Failed to connect 28) return 0 ;; # Operation timeout 35) return 0 ;; # SSL connect error 52) return 0 ;; # Empty reply 56) return 0 ;; # Network receive error esac # Check stderr for common transient messages if [[ "$stderr" =~ (timeout|timed.out|connection.refused|temporarily) ]]; then return 0 fi return 1 # Not retryable } ``` ### Integration with fetch_and_run() ```bash verified_fetch_and_run() { local url="$1" local checksum="$2" local name="$3" shift 3 retry_with_backoff \ "verify_checksum \"$url\" \"$checksum\" \"$name\"" \ "fetching $name" local status=$? if ((status \!= 0)); then log_error "Failed to fetch $name after ${#RETRY_DELAYS[@]} attempts" return 1 fi # Content already verified, now run verified_content | bash -s -- "$@" } ``` ## Non-Retryable Scenarios - HTTP 404 (script doesnt exist) - HTTP 403 (permission denied) - Checksum mismatch (not a network issue) - Script execution errors ## Dependencies - Used by: security.sh fetch functions - Used by: install.sh download operations ## Acceptance Criteria -
